# Cumulative Computing

> A computational paradigm in which systems accumulate work over time by preserving artifacts that represent completed computation.

Cumulative Computing proposes that computational infrastructure should preserve **computational work** — not just data. When artifacts produced by computation persist, the work they represent persists. When they disappear, the work must be performed again.

The framework is developed through two primary works:

## The Paradigm

- [Cumulative Computing](https://cumulativecomputing.org/cumulative-computing/): The manifesto introducing Cumulative Computing as a paradigm — the problem of forgetting, how human knowledge systems solved it, and what it means for machine computation.

## AAA Framework

The Agent Artifact Availability (AAA) framework defines the reliability property required to support Cumulative Computing systems. Seven technical notes develop the framework from first principles.

- [01 — Agent Artifact Availability](https://cumulativecomputing.org/framework/01/): Introduces the AAA reliability property: artifacts must remain retrievable, verifiable, reusable, and persistent across agents, workflows, and time.

- [02 — Artifacts as Units of Work](https://cumulativecomputing.org/framework/02/): Establishes that artifacts are not merely data — they are durable representations of completed computational work.

- [03 — Artifact Graphs](https://cumulativecomputing.org/framework/03/): Artifacts depend on prior artifacts, forming directed graphs that encode the structure and history of computational work.

- [04 — Why Storage Systems Fail](https://cumulativecomputing.org/framework/04/): Analyzes why traditional storage systems — filesystems, databases, object storage — fail to preserve artifact graphs and therefore lose computational work.

- [05 — Artifact Availability Layer](https://cumulativecomputing.org/framework/05/): Proposes a new infrastructure layer between compute and storage that manages artifact identity, discovery, graph preservation, and availability.

- [06 — Deterministic Artifact Identity](https://cumulativecomputing.org/framework/06/): Derives that stable artifact identity must be computed deterministically from the computation and inputs that produced the artifact.

- [07 — Computational Work Conservation](https://cumulativecomputing.org/framework/07/): States the culminating principle: completed computational work persists only through the artifacts produced by that work. Systems that preserve artifact availability accumulate work over time.

## Key Concepts

- **Artifact**: The durable output of a completed computational process
- **Artifact Graph**: The directed graph of artifacts and their derivation relationships
- **Artifact Identity**: A stable identifier derived from the computation and inputs that produced an artifact
- **Artifact Availability**: The property that artifacts remain retrievable, verifiable, and reusable across agents, workflows, and time
- **Computational Work**: The accumulated structure of computation encoded in an artifact graph
- **Computational Work Conservation**: Completed computational work persists only through the artifacts produced by that work
- **Cumulative Computing**: The paradigm in which systems accumulate computational work over time rather than repeatedly destroying and recreating it

## Author

Rich Kopcho — [GitHub: kopcho/AAA](https://github.com/kopcho/AAA)
